speaker: interviewer
text: --4. What was the rest of your journey like back to Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was slow, and it was convoluted. And we finally arrived in Holland, and we were placed in another quarantine-- and I think this one was three weeks-- in the southern part of Holland. And it was to make sure that we didn't show up with TB or anything like that. And then we finally were allowed to journey back to Amsterdam. I do not remember, in any way, the reunion with my uncle. I only know it was. He had been hidden during the war. But when the Germans had picked us up, and when I had not allowed to be put in the coal bin, he stayed in bed. And when the Germans came to our apartment-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I remember they-- they-- they mowed down flowers that my mother had gotten from-- for-- for her birthday from my father-- my uncle said he can't move. He's paralyzed. And he did have sort of a brace, and he had a cane. And he said there's no way that he can walk. And so they said they would pick him up. And then, of course, he took off, and he went underground. So my uncle survived that way. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we stayed first for a while with friends of my parents who had also lived in the same building where we did before the war. And they had escaped to Switzerland, and they had come back. And they were very, very caring for us. And my mother and I lived with them for a while until my uncle was able to get an apartment, which, oddly enough, was in the same entrance that we lived in before except one story lower, the second floor. And then my mother and my uncle and I moved to that apartment, and again, a room was rented out to a survivor from Auschwitz who lived with us, Dr. Benjamin. And at that point I had to go back to grammar school because I had missed so much. So I was one year in grammar school, same building that the other school had been in, and then went to the Amsterdam-- Amsterdams Lyceum, which was a very good kind of a-- a high school, combination of regular high school and gymnasium. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] At that point, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I started-- I can only-- when I look at it now, I would say I started life with a vengeance. I wanted to have a good time. I had lots of friends. We did stupid stuff, I mean, silly stuff. We-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it-- it was just almost-- you know, I was 12 at the time, 13. I just wanted to have a good time, and I didn't want to have any responsibilities. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wanted to have fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever talk about the camp and your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never, never, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not to your mother? Not to your Uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and I didn't talk about it. My uncle didn't ask me any questions. Nobody asked. I think those who weren't in camps didn't want to know. Those who were in camps didn't want to talk. I looked for friends that were not related in any way, that I didn't know their history, nobody from before the war that-- none-- one of the friends who did come back from before the war came back without her parents. I didn't want anything to do with her. I just very purposefully, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sort of, you know, in a-- in a childlike way, put a line under it and said, that goes in the box, and we go from now. The only thing-- and again, that-- that comes back under-- under some of the disillusionments-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one of my first trips when we came back from-- from the camps was to find out where these twins were who had my doll. And I went to their apartment and I said, hi, I'm here for my doll. And I got this funny look, which, to this day, I don't quite explain or can't or don't want to, and they don't have the doll. And maybe this doll was too ugly to bring back. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they had promised me that they'd bring back the doll, and they didn't. And then I started sort of questioning all of a sudden, how come they were allowed to stay in this camp with my doll, you know, where all the children had to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I got an explanation from either my uncle or from my mother that the father was involved with making up the lists of the people who were going to go on transport. And of course, that's one job that is essential. You don't find too many volunteers for that. But that-- that came much later, you know. At the time, I only remember that they hadn't brought my doll back.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I do remember that everybody was searching frantically for family members. You know, there was this constant, if somebody came back, oh, did you know my relative? This seemed to be going on among the grown-ups. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I know that my mother asked these questions to other survivors and-- because my father hadn't come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, somehow or other, I think the message was given to me that my father wasn't going to come back, and I didn't believe that. And I remember sitting, especially when I still was in the regular-- in the grammar school, waiting for the door to open probably for a year. And it didn't, of course. I thought he was maybe a prisoner in-- in Russia somewhere, you know, anything but. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And apparently-- I-- I just looked in this-- this diary that I started in '47-- at that point, I had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] accepted the fact that my father wasn't going to come back, because I wrote it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But in the beginning, I-- I just expected him to appear, you know, just a little later than the rest. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So otherwise, I-- I-- I can only say that I spent the first three years of-- of this-- the-- the lyceum life almost in a frivolous way. I did my homework, but the emphasis was on-- on sports, on going to the dances, on-- on having a good time. Did you write anything besides your diaries after the war? Did you keep any records of things that had happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I wrote down, at one point, and I don't remember where, all the songs and poems and-- and all those [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] things that I remembered about the camp. And there were songs. In Westerbork, actually, I was in a-- in a choir that sang for the commandant, including one Hebrew song. And I still don't-- I can't forget that one. And also, we sang a German song.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to say any of those songs now on tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't. [LAUGHS] I really don't. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- in-- in-- in both Westerbork and in-- in Theresienstadt, there was, at one time, sort of like a cabaret. I mean, there was an awful lot of pretend, and there was, I think, a lot of grown-ups apparently needed to find their escapes. And so whatever I was exposed to and-- and remembered, shortly after the war I must have written it down. And some of it is-- is very almost tongue in cheek. There was a-- a Westerbork alphabet poem that each letter described a camp condition. And it was done in a semi-humorous way rather than totally heavy, but the heaviness comes through anyway. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There-- there were some songs that were sung in German in Theresienstadt, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some of them just showed some of the relationships that went on between men and women, and apparently these continued. And some-- there was-- is one that is-- is very sad. And-- and, you know, when I look at it now, then I realize that people wrote this, and they knew something. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's in German. I can try to translate it. It's called 'Ade, Day Kamerad,' 'Good-bye, Comrade.' [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Here splits our thread, because tomorrow I have to leave. I will part from you. One drives me from here. I am going on the Poland transport. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Wait a minute. You were so good to me. You gave me much courage. You were always ready to help. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I had-- what do you call it-- when you pressed my hand, it took the worries away. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We suffered together the suffering. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Ade, Kamerad, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I feel sorry for you. And this parting [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] is very difficult for me. It's heavy. You were so good for me. You gave me much courage. And now we will never see each other again. And, oddly enough, I wrote under there the date that my father was transported. So, I mean, that's not one of the lighter songs. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I could-- I know the melody, but I don't trust myself to sing it. I don't think I'll ever forget the melody. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I think probably what made it-- the-- my father's death especially difficult is that we didn't talk about it. My mother and I didn't share it. It wasn't cried about. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My grandfather, who I was very attached to, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it was like a total disappearance rather than a cut. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think that it, therefore, it just lingered longer. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I-- apparently from-- from what I reread-- I-- I did a lot of searching yesterday-- accepted, at one level, that my father had died in 1947. But I wouldn't say Kaddish for him. I didn't say Kaddish for him till [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] probably 20 years later. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just-- I just couldn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, now I do. But-- so there was-- there-- there is a certain-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] that it didn't really end. OK? It just simmered on and on. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this whole camp period, I mean, not talking about. When we moved to America, nobody knew about it. When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved to America in 1949. I remember a story that my mother told me. She-- my mother visited America in 1948. And again, it was around the time of her birthday, and my uncle, who had been living in America since, you know, '38 or '37, had given her for her birthday a-- a bracelet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were in some sort of social company, you know, a social gathering, and somebody asked her how was it for her during the war. And my mother said, well, I was in a concentration camp. And the next question-- this is the way what my mother related to me-- was, well, did you save that bracelet?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother was so aghast at this comeback that she said to me, don't ever talk about it. People don't understand. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you know, people don't understand. I didn't understand either. I thought I had to go back and look and to-- to make sure that this was real. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- we came to America. And again, you know, I-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I tried to have a good time and, in a way, accomplished that. I went to school here. I finished high school. I went to college, became a dental hygienist. Nobody ever knew my background.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one incident where the-- there were the drawings from the children in Theresienstadt. There was an exhibit here in-- in the city. And some other friends said, let's go and take our kids. And I said, no, I'm not interested. And I got this lecture of, you know, we owe that to our children. And they tried to really make me feel bad about denying this part of-- of-- of Jewish life, life. And then my husband finally told them, you know, Judy was in this camp. She can't go. And they were-- I mean, it would have been a lot better if I would have said why I couldn't go. So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it-- it really-- it's-- it remained an unfinished business for a long, long time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It came up again in a totally unexpected form when one of my kids started having some difficulties leaving the house. And at that point, I was told to go for some help, which I did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at that point, I got, again, this rage that I had this time with the German soldiers of this isn't fair. You know, here we go again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had-- you know, what is this? I've made a-- a line under it already, and you know, how dare this come back again? And-- and I remember thinking that the Germans won after all. They won by having those killed that they killed and those maimed that survived and that they really had won the battle. So at that point, I-- I had need for some intensive therapy, and we weren't in a position to foot those bills.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is-- at that point, I finally [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] relented the-- the request that my uncle in Holland had been making year after year to file for restitution. Because I thought for us to go broke and have this happen to the children, this-- this reaction, that just isn't right. So then I thought I'm going to get out of Germany whatever I can. But on the other hand, for instance, even now I was invited by the city that my mom comes from, Montabaur, to visit. Mm-mm, I'm not going to say thank you to them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So there is still-- there's still unfinished business. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There is unfinished business about not having a-- a cemetery to go to. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There is unfinished business because I'm not able to portray my father to my children because it's difficult to talk to me-- talk about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- for years-- I mean, there is in the literature the survival guilt. Well, for years I went around with the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] thought, feeling, that if I wouldn't have existed, my father would have lived, because it was because I was a child that he had to leave. Otherwise he could have stayed. And it occurred to me only much later that I didn't ask to be born either. But, I mean, originally I focused on if I hadn't been around, my father would be alive now, and my mother would have a husband. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think everybody finds some way of finding an explanation for feeling guilty about surviving. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think that probably nobody, if you want to be totally [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bottom line about it, nobody who came back could come back without somebody else not going under. Any transport that we stayed off of, somebody else went on. So that-- that doesn't feel good. But I-- it took a very personal tone with me with-- with the feelings that I had about my dad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't really-- I don't think I've ever been able to really talk to the kids about this period. One of the reasons that I really want to do the tape is for posterity, but also for my personal posterity for the children. And-- and there have been remarks-- so now we will learn what this is going to be, what this was like. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] At this point, I feel almost that we have an obligation to talk, and I do volunteer to go to schools and talk about it. But it's much easier to talk to strangers, strange kids, and answer their questions. And I usually tell them, you can ask me anything you want. You know, I'll answer unless I really feel that it's not appropriate. It doesn't give me difficulty. I can go back and have lunch. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's just the-- the kind of an experience that I think is so out of the-- the realm of experiences that it maybe is-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you have to learn to accept it, that it's part of you, rather than denying it. And once it becomes part of you, it becomes a sad part of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that doesn't mean that you can't, you know, look, it's sunshine out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I enjoy my family. I enjoy my work, love my husband, love my family, my grandchildren. So I'm not living life in the past, but it is part of me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and every once in a while-- I mean, I used to-- if I was driving my car and-- and the-- the railroad gate would go down and there would be a freight train, I mean, I would shiver. I'd have to turn on the radio and-- and think of-- of different things. That's not the case anymore. I mean, I can stand there now and say, oh, boy, this is holding me up. I got to be at work, which is a normal response. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Every once in a while, there's still something that'll flash it on unexpectedly, and-- and that I can't handle very well. I went back to Westerbork this past May, because that's the first time that I was able to-- to do that. I am not able, yet, to go to the Holocaust Museum. I-- I just don't want to be surprised by anything that I'm not protected against. So you know, I know there's still some very sensitive spots about this. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] But by and large, people do not know my history, you know, people in my work life or-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think, you know, some of our friends treat it probably very respectfully. You don't get intrusive questions, and I like it that way. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I really-- the idea of having to spend the rest of my life with this weighing me down is a-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it-- it's a horrible thought, you know. So I-- in a way, I almost have to look forward. And most of the time I do. OK? What is your relationship to Judaism today?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Really mixed. As I said, it took me a good 20 years to say the Kaddish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have a real push-pull relationship. I couldn't be blindly Orthodox anymore. I question.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oddly enough, my son, when he-- he became bar mitzvah, his speech consisted strictly of questions, why? Why did God let this happen? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And our rabbi, who is very demanding when it comes to bar mitzvah speeches and usually has the kids rewrite them over and over again, he just let this fly. But his whole speech was almost an accusation. And I-- I think he-- he might have been talking for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel it is extremely important that Judaism survive. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But I have great ambivalence in-- in-- in seeing God up there as a all-kind figure, you know. So I-- I-- I have very mixed feelings. When I go to synagogue, it makes me feel good, though, because I like the ritual and the closeness and the-- the-- you're sort of borne by-- by other people, you know, who are all in this together. So that I-- I do like. I once went back in Holland to the synagogue that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the area where I lived, and I remember that I cried and cried and cried. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So some of it is-- is really a-- almost like a sad-- some of the part of religion is a sad remembrance of-- of things that are lost, rather than what-- what religion really means. It has more to do with memory.
